 {"id":"franken_networkx-2u4","title":"Extract flow algorithm epsilon values to named constants","description":"## What

Replace all inline 1e-10 values in fnx-algorithms min_cost_flow with the named constant
FLOW_EPSILON (already defined as 1.0e-10 at the top of lib.rs).

## Status: COMPLETED (March 2026)

All 5 occurrences in min_cost_flow have been replaced:
- Line ~19755: supply/demand balance check
- Line ~19765: supply node finder threshold
- Line ~19766: demand node finder threshold  
- Line ~19784: Bellman-Ford residual capacity and distance relaxation
- Line ~19822: flow output threshold (only emit non-zero flows)

## Why FLOW_EPSILON specifically

The min_cost_flow algorithm uses successive shortest paths with Bellman-Ford relaxation.
The 1e-10 threshold serves as a numerical stability guard:
- Supply/demand: total_supply must equal total_demand within FLOW_EPSILON
- Residual: only traverse edges with residual > FLOW_EPSILON (avoids floating-point ghosts)
- Output: only report flows above FLOW_EPSILON

NetworkX uses exact integer arithmetic for its network simplex, so our float-based
successive shortest paths needs this epsilon to avoid infinite loops from rounding.

## Files Changed

- crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs (lines 25, ~19755, ~19765, ~19766, ~19784, ~19822)

## Verification

- cargo test -p fnx-algorithms passes (595 tests)
- Python test suite passes (1082+ tests)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:03:31.541611556Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-25T03:04:01.146759523Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T03:04:01.146045561Z","close_reason":"All 5 inline 1e-10 values replaced with FLOW_EPSILON constant. Verified: 595 Rust tests + 1082 Python tests pass.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"franken_networkx-4587","title":"NX-REPLACE: Centrality extensions — load, percolation, second_order, group, subset variants","description":"## Functions (10) — centrality algorithm extensions
1. load_centrality (__init__.py:3227) — like betweenness but counts all shortest paths through node
2. percolation_centrality (__init__.py:5762) — requires node percolation states
3. second_order_centrality (__init__.py:5776) — random walk variance
4. group_betweenness_centrality (__init__.py:5839) — betweenness for a GROUP of nodes
5. group_closeness_centrality (__init__.py:5850) — closeness for a group
6. betweenness_centrality_subset (__init__.py:5720) — betweenness restricted to source/target subsets
7. edge_betweenness_centrality_subset (__init__.py:5729) — edge variant of above
8. information_centrality (__init__.py:5771) — current-flow based
9. edge_load_centrality (__init__.py:5738) — load per edge
10. communicability_betweenness_centrality (__init__.py:5790) — matrix exponential based

## Implementation tiers
Tier A (reuse existing infrastructure):
- load_centrality: variant of betweenness counting, shares shortest-path code
- group_betweenness/closeness: extend existing single-node centrality with group aggregation
- subset variants: restrict existing betweenness to source/target subsets

Tier B (new algorithms):
- percolation_centrality: needs node state handling + modified shortest path
- second_order_centrality: random walk standard deviation computation
- information_centrality: current-flow based (needs pseudo-inverse or LU solve)

Tier C (numpy-dependent, consider keeping NX):
- communicability_betweenness: matrix exponential — may keep as NX

## Testing requirements
Create tests/python/test_centrality_extensions_parity.py:
- test_load_centrality_matches_nx: compare on 3+ graphs
- test_load_centrality_normalized: verify sum == 1.0 when normalized
- test_percolation_centrality_matches_nx: set states, compare
- test_second_order_centrality_matches_nx: compare on connected graph
- test_group_betweenness_matches_nx: group={0,1}, compare value
- test_group_closeness_matches_nx: group={0,1}, compare value
- test_betweenness_subset_matches_nx: sources={0}, targets={3}, compare
- test_edge_betweenness_subset_matches_nx: same setup
- test_information_centrality_matches_nx: compare on small connected graph
- test_edge_load_centrality_matches_nx: compare edge values

Each test: build graph, run both fnx and nx, assert values within 1e-10.
Run: pytest tests/python/test_centrality_extensions_parity.py -v --tb=long

## Files to modify
- crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs: implement 7-9 centrality variants
- crates/fnx-python/src/algorithms.rs: add bindings
- python/franken_networkx/__init__.py: rewire functions
- tests/python/test_centrality_extensions_parity.py: new test file","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-03T00:19:13.447751417Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-03T20:08:36.603155844Z","closed_at":"2026-04-03T20:08:36.602760637Z","close_reason":"Completed centrality wrapper/native tranche; rewired safe Rust-backed paths, fixed percolation_centrality attribute parity, and split remaining algorithms to franken_networkx-1gmi","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"franken_networkx-8aw","title":"Track Python multigraph limitation notes as future work","description":"## What

Three docstrings in python/franken_networkx/__init__.py contain the note
'multigraphs not yet supported' in the context of pandas conversion functions:

1. to_pandas_edgelist (line ~7791): edge_key parameter ignored
2. from_pandas_edgelist (line ~7866): edge_key parameter ignored
3. to_pandas_adjacency (line ~7913): edge_key parameter ignored

These are NOT bugs — they're accurate API limitation documentation. However,
they represent a parity gap with NetworkX which does support multigraph
pandas conversion.

## Context

FrankenNetworkX already supports MultiGraph and MultiDiGraph types with full
method parity (34 methods + 6 view types). The gap is specifically in the
pandas conversion helpers which don't handle the edge_key parameter for
disambiguating parallel edges.

## What Would Be Needed

1. to_pandas_edgelist: include edge key column when G.is_multigraph()
2. from_pandas_edgelist: read edge_key column to reconstruct MultiGraph
3. to_pandas_adjacency: unclear NX semantics for multigraphs (low priority)

## Priority

P3 (low) — pandas interop with multigraphs is a niche use case. The core
multigraph functionality works fine.

## No Code Changes

This bead is a tracking placeholder. Close it when multigraph pandas
support is implemented or explicitly descoped.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:06:44.860119526Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-25T03:23:45.576348796Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T03:23:45.575600410Z","close_reason":"Tracked as known limitation.  {"id":"franken_networkx-cm5","title":"Generators: Scale-free variants (dual BA, extended BA, scale_free_graph)","description":"Generators: Scale-Free Graph Variants

Extended preferential attachment and power-law graph generators beyond basic Barabási-Albert.

## Functions to Implement

- `dual_barabasi_albert_graph(n, m1, m2, p, initial_graph=None, seed=None) -> Graph`: BA variant where each new node attaches m1 edges with probability p, or m2 edges with probability (1-p). Models heterogeneous attachment rates.
- `extended_barabasi_albert_graph(n, m, p, q, initial_graph=None, seed=None) -> Graph`: BA variant with three operations per step: add node+edges (prob p), add edges between existing (prob q), rewire existing edge (prob 1-p-q).
- `scale_free_graph(n, alpha=0.41, beta=0.54, gamma=0.05, delta_in=0.2, delta_out=0, initial_graph=None, seed=None) -> MultiDiGraph`: Directed scale-free graph using Bollobás et al. model with in/out degree preferential attachment.
- `random_powerlaw_tree(n, gamma=3, seed=None, tries=100) -> Graph`: Generate a random tree with power-law degree distribution using a pruned configuration model.
- `random_powerlaw_tree_sequence(n, gamma=3, seed=None, tries=100) -> list`: Generate a degree sequence for a power-law tree (sum=2(n-1), valid tree sequence).
- `gn_graph(n, kernel=None, create_using=None, seed=None) -> DiGraph`: Growing network (GN) graph where new nodes attach to one existing node chosen proportional to kernel(degree). Default kernel is identity (linear preferential attachment).

## Algorithmic Approach

- Dual BA: extend existing Rust BA engine with dual-mode attachment selection per step.
- Extended BA: three-phase step logic (add-node, add-edge, rewire) with preferential attachment for target selection.
- scale_free_graph: Bollobás directed model - maintain in-degree and out-degree lists, select source/target with probability proportional to degree + delta offset.
- random_powerlaw_tree: generate power-law degree sequence via `powerlaw_sequence`, check validity (sum=2(n-1), all ≥1), build tree via Prüfer sequence or configuration model. Retry up to `tries` times.
- gn_graph: sequential node addition, each new node connects to one existing node with probability proportional to kernel(in_degree).

## Performance Targets

- Extend existing Rust BA infrastructure where possible for O(n·m) construction.
- scale_free_graph: O(n) expected time using degree-list sampling.
- random_powerlaw_tree: may need multiple tries; each try is O(n).
- Target: 100K node dual/extended BA graphs in < 2 seconds.

## Edge Cases

- `p + q > 1` in extended BA → raise ValueError.
- `m1 == m2` in dual BA → degenerates to standard BA.
- `alpha + beta + gamma != 1` in scale_free_graph → raise ValueError.
- `initial_graph` with fewer nodes than required (need at least m+1 for BA variants).
- `random_powerlaw_tree` failing to find valid sequence after `tries` → raise NetworkXError.
- `n = 0` or `n = 1` edge cases.
- `kernel=None` defaults to identity function in gn_graph.

## Test Requirements

- Unit tests: correct node/edge counts, graph type (directed vs undirected).
- Degree distribution tests: fit power-law exponent on large graphs (n≥10000), verify γ is within expected range.
- Reproducibility tests: same seed → identical graph (node-for-node, edge-for-edge).
- NX cross-validation: compare with NetworkX output for identical seeds and parameters.
- Property tests: dual BA with p=1 equivalent to BA(m1), p=0 equivalent to BA(m2).
- Extended BA: verify all three operations occur with approximately correct frequencies over many steps.
- scale_free_graph: verify in-degree and out-degree distributions are both power-law.
- random_powerlaw_tree: output is a tree (connected, n-1 edges).

## Implementation Notes

- Extend existing Rust BA implementation in the crate (reuse preferential attachment sampling).
- Python wrapper layer for API compatibility.
- scale_free_graph returns MultiDiGraph (parallel edges possible).
- gn_graph is a directed graph model.
- Depends on: core graph types, existing BA Rust infrastructure, numpy for random sampling.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-21T19:20:21.711622106Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-23T06:11:17.367892098Z","closed_at":"2026-03-23T06:11:17.367368888Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["generators","tier-2"]}

 {"id":"franken_networkx-rns8","title":"PERF: Enforce performance SLO gates with automated regression detection","description":"## Status assessment
Significant infrastructure already exists:
- scripts/run_benchmark_gate.sh: runs baseline matrix, hotspot backlog, regression gate
- scripts/run_perf_baseline_matrix.py: multi-run benchmarks with percentiles
- artifacts/perf/: baseline data, CGSE benchmarks, phase2c data, latest snapshots
- artifacts/perf/cgse/: CGSE policy bench with p50/p95/p99 data
- CI workflow (ci.yml) has G6 placeholder for perf gate

## What's MISSING (the actual gap)
1. **SLO threshold file**: No machine-readable file mapping spec SLOs to thresholds:
   - shortest-path p95 ≤ 420ms
   - component p95 ≤ 280ms
   - centrality p95 ≤ 950ms
   - flow p95 ≤ 700ms
   - I/O ≥ 45 MB/s
   - mutation regression ≤ +8%
   - memory regression ≤ +10%
   - p99 tail ≤ +10%

2. **Automated SLO comparison**: The benchmark scripts produce data but don't compare against thresholds. Need a script that reads SLO file + benchmark output → pass/fail.

3. **CI integration**: The CI workflow has the structure but the perf gate needs to run scripts/run_benchmark_gate.sh and check exit code.

## Implementation plan
1. Create artifacts/perf/slo_thresholds.json with all 8 spec thresholds
2. Add SLO comparison logic to scripts/run_benchmark_gate.sh (exit non-zero if exceeded)
3. Wire into CI G6 step

## Testing
- Verify benchmark scripts run and produce output
- Verify SLO check passes on current code
- Verify SLO check would fail on artificially slow code

## Acceptance criteria
- All 8 SLO thresholds defined in machine-readable format
- Benchmark gate exits non-zero if any SLO exceeded
- CI G6 runs the gate","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-06T15:49:31.293787886Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-07T01:19:47.606612228Z","closed_at":"2026-04-07T01:19:47.606326918Z","close_reason":"Implemented SLO gate: created slo_thresholds.json with all 8 spec thresholds, run_perf_slo_gate.py comparison script, wired into benchmark pipeline. 3/8 regression SLOs enforced now, 5 absolute SLOs auto-activate when algorithm-family benchmarks are added.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"franken_networkx-rns8","depends_on_id":"franken_networkx-zrlb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-06T15:50:45.472672066Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"franken_networkx-vji","title":"EPIC: Hardcoded Constants & Code Hygiene Sweep","description":"## Overview

Comprehensive audit of the FrankenNetworkX codebase found several categories of issues:
1. Hardcoded magic numbers that should be named constants for maintainability and correctness
2. Inline epsilon values inconsistent with existing named constants
3. Generator safety bounds that need documentation/justification
4. Python docstring limitations that need tracking
5. Conformance/dispatch layer constants that need review

This epic tracks the full remediation of all findings from the March 2026 audit.

## Context

FrankenNetworkX is a Rust port of NetworkX with PyO3 Python bindings. The codebase has
387+ Rust algorithms in fnx-algorithms, 22 generators in fnx-generators, and 1082+ Python
tests. The project uses a strict/hardened mode split where numerical thresholds directly
affect correctness and compatibility with NetworkX's behavioral oracle.

## Why This Matters

- Magic numbers make it impossible to tune thresholds without grep-and-pray
- Inconsistent epsilon values across similar computations cause subtle parity drift
- Named constants enable centralized tuning for different precision/performance tradeoffs
- Self-documenting constants reduce onboarding friction for new contributors

## Completion Criteria

- Zero inline magic numbers in non-test Rust code
- All epsilon/threshold values use named constants with doc comments
- Generator bounds documented with rationale
- Python limitation notes tracked as beads for future
